#🌐︱general-chat 17:52:54 jimcollinson
I think the thought process behind it could be summarised as creating a model that gives: **Built-in Security:** So mass-bot tactics are expensive and impractical, so you know results come from real voters. **A stronger turnout**: Because it aims to widen appeal, adds some excitement, and gives something back for people having… **Skin in the Game**: Because you’re not just clicking a button; you’re investing attention, belief, and a little risk. Along with… **A Stronger Ecosystem:** Because, a little risk filters out weak ideas early, so the overall network grows with real builders and real believers.
View in Discord →

#🌐︱general-chat 10:48:53 jimcollinson
Well, there's lots going on in that thread, but much of the interpretation there was based on a metric for popularity, which was not part of the criteria set out in the T&Cs. We couldn't post-hoc change that criteria, or overlay a popularity metric over the top in the same way, as had this been set out prior, it would have likely changed voting patterns a lot. In the end, there was only one project that had a low number of unique voters, and one voter with a 68% share of that vote. None of the rest was clear cut. If you want follow-up discussion, let's do so in the forum, as it's easier to follow along.

#🌐︱general-chat 11:23:16 jimcollinson
At the moment, if you locked 1 ANT, that would get you 1.998 pledge points that you can pledge to an app. If that app deploys, then you'll be in for a share of the rewards pool, which is split amongst all backers of successful apps, proportional to how much they backed.

#🌐︱general-chat 11:24:16 jimcollinson

View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 14:49:07 jimcollinson
Actually, that's even easier. If you look at the leaderboard, you'll see total pledge points, your pledge points, and then your current split of the reward pool. Now bear in mind, this is as if _all_ the projects deployed successfully. We hope they all will of course, but if some don't, and you backed ones that did, it means that total would be larger!
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 15:35:50 jimcollinson
Take this basic example: Rewards Pool = 50,000 ANT Total Pledge Points = 600,000 Your Pledged Points = 90,000 Your % of pool = 15% If all projects deployed, you'd get = **7500 ANT** along with your 90,000 ANT unlocked on 13 June 2026. Make sense?
👍 1
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 15:40:10 jimcollinson
A further example. Let's say the above is true, but you pledged all your 90,000 on one app, and that turned out to be the only one to deploy. And there is a total of 100,000 points pledged against that one app. Your 90k plus another 10k from others. You'd have 90% of the pool. So **45,000 ANT** rewards. Plus probably some decent cash prizes too, if you pledged that backing during the first week!
View in Discord →

📅 Tuesday, 2025-06-03
#🐛︱bug-reports 12:44:16 jimcollinson
Just had a good ol' discussion about this. Really the docs should say 256 _or_ 128. In the end it doesn't really matter to much at all to the level of security or encryption we end up with… because that part isn't really doing the encryption, more the randomisation. Either 256, or 128, and you still end up with an effective one-time pad / information-theoretically secure system.
❤️ 2

📅 Monday, 2025-06-09
#🎙︱discussion-topics 15:22:49 jimcollinson
Folks, love the passion here — we all want to see Autonomi grow. As a small team, we’ve been working consistently and steadily every day, building with integrity and no big VC money. There are no shortcuts here, no pump and dumps, no fakery, no illusions: just honest, consistent effort for the long term. This is a community project, and it thrives when we all pitch in. If you have a network or a voice, help spread the word and bring others along. Together, we’re building something that lasts 💪

We've long had some every interesting designs to combat junk email — and it's more elegant than pay ... [truncated]

View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 16:16:20 jimcollinson
I personally believe a new type/form of messaging will form — something neither email nor IMs will do. Rather than a patch-up job for email. Particularly when it comes to identity, trust, and authenticity.

View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 17:32:15 traktion0_20257 → replied to JimCollinson
jimcollinson said:
We've long had some every interesting designs to combat junk email — and it's more elegant than pay ... [truncated]
saying that, we already must spend tokens to persist data on the network (unless re-using a scratchpad), so I suspect we would already have that 'feature' to deter spammers. Even if it is dust costs, it would stop spam dead - they need emails to be essentially free to make it worth it
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 17:34:55 jimcollinson
Sctrachpads I imagine will be a key part of things TBH. And eyah you are correct on the data costs, but they are trivial really. And you get the same kind of spam problems with other paid messaging, like SMS… becuase the ROI remains good enough
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 17:38:54 jimcollinson
You could, though: - Screen messages from first time senders (Hey! Email has a great version of this) - Think about _staking_ for messaging for new senders to up the risk for spammers. - Have robust controls and permissions around access via dIDs etc (way more scope there than email) - and more!

#🌐︱general-chat 23:41:26 janeytheterrible → replied to JimCollinson
jimcollinson said:
I personally believe a new type/form of messaging will form — something neither email nor IMs will d ... [truncated]
I agree that a much improved mail system will emerge, but remember that mail has gone through millennia of innovation and email was also an evolution of people trying to improve on the simple concept of mail/letters. I do think the mail concept that has been evolving for 1000's of years will stand the test of time, and the mail system for Autonomi will be able to improve on email but fundamentally will not change the concept of mail. Many have tried over the last 100's of years and have fallen by the wayside. email came out of early attempts in the 60's to have messages across ARPANET and by about 1970 or 71 email was basically in place. So even after 50 years people have not been able to make significant changes and survive. The era of short messages will remain since that is like passing notes. And that was the best alternative that has survived because at first phones could not do long messages and passing notes is a long held tradition of the youth, (school anyone) and most others.

#🌐︱general-chat 15:33:11 mckissick → replied to JimCollinson
jimcollinson said:
Sure! Right here, or maybe over on the forum if it is longer form perhaps. What ideas do you have?
Thanks @JimCollinson for suggesting this. I did post about the "New Money System for the Future - IOUBI" in the "Related Projects" forum and got a couple comments. But I think there could be a much bigger offering to people with more tools working together. Having a perfected, decentralized money to offer would attract tons of users but imagine if it solved other things really well too. The addons I propose included with the monetary system, could help people solve governance, taxes, advertising, insurance, UBI, greed, crime, data privacy and more. When added to the advancement we're on track for (aside from computer based solutions), we seem ready to get a whole host of the new future very soon. To give a preview, imagine lots of things becoming abundant. By abundant, I mean that we'll have as much as we realistically want, available for near zero marginal cost. Things like energy, transportation, food diversity/supply, information, privacy, housing, land, virtually everything to run the 99% expected parts of society. Unfortunately, I don't see everyone getting their own free yacht and helicopter fleet. But the rest, that's all definitely well in progress. The only roadblock is most of those technologies need a ubiquitous currency with no centralized or functional problems, a singular interface to 'the net', and a few other things - all that Autonomi is posed to provide. Let's discuss
View in Discord →

📅 Monday, 2025-10-27
#🌐︱general-chat 11:04:12 jimcollinson
Paymasters is really a tool, part of a tool kit, that we give to developers that offer lots of flexibility as to how their apps can work for end users. - At its most basic: it can allow someone else to fund the gas for the end user - Or it can allow the user to only hold ant and pay for both with that - Taking it further the app developer could cover both and have that sorted for the end user and then charge them any which way they like (subscription, one-time fee, promotions, credit card payments etc) - but all the while the user still is the one in charge of their data. The roadmap items for credit card payments, or agentic payments, are separate things again; but all aim to offer options to developers and remove friction for end users.
❤️ 6 👏 2
View in Discord →

I think data wallet is actually a fairly useful and elegant term (not sure of the use of "agentic wallets" at this point though!)

*"Network emissions, as some will know, are currently suppressed from intended levels. At some point in the near future, they will increase in order to realign with the emissions schedule that is outlined in the white paper (so they can perform their intended role, which is to support capacity growth and consistent earnings, as more data starts to flow into the network/onto the nodes)."* *" We have now prioritised a remedy for this exploitive and potentially damaging behaviour, and will be rolling the same out asap. #🎙︱discussion-topics 17:19:34 mightyfool0689 → replied to JimCollinson
jimcollinson said:
others can pile in for the deep analysis, I suppose, but remembering the nodes won't just dramatical ... [truncated]
This is still one thing that I’m not sure about. I do think the nodes will fill up drastically, because node operators are heavily overprovisioning at the moment. Meaning that once the network starts to fill (even if slowly) there will come a point where node operators will have to scale down their nodes to be able to deliver the storage space required, triggering an event of other nodes filling up more and triggering another wave of scaling down. I’m expecting to see this as early as 1,5% node capacity used.

#🎙︱discussion-topics 09:48:26 jimcollinson
Or let's think of this in terms of the user experience that it becomes possible to build when we have things like paymasters and the Merkel tree improvements to gas payments or even things like fiat payments that become possible in short order: The background Sync potential! I have a directory that I lump files into on my desktop. It is connected with my wallet where I have my token or even I bought data credits with my credit card or it's linked to another fiat payment mechanism and it sits there syncing those files to the autonomi network in the background, overnight, or just trickles onto the network without intervention. I don't need to deal with quotes, it's not a manual process, and I even combo with a node which is topping things up to. If we start to think about Applications on the web as local first, with permanent background storage sync, there's some really cool things we can do.

jimcollinson said:
Or let's think of this in terms of the user experience that it becomes possible to build when we hav ... [truncated]
fwiw, this is the direction IMIM (with AntTP) is heading in too. You publish content, which appears on your blog instantly. In the background, it is synced to the network. From the UX perspective, adding a new article on your blog is instant. In reality, it is shared with the rest of the world a little later, but the user isn't sat waiting for that. There will be UI queues to illustrate sync status too.

#🌐︱general-chat 16:53:39 jimcollinson

#🌐︱general-chat 17:00:35 jimcollinson
I'll try and answer what I think you're asking! Again, I'm not the expert here, that would be Lajos… hopefully he can jump in and correct me if I'm wrong. When someone initiates a gasless transaction in dave, - The PM the cost of the gas in ETH and gets the exchange rate from an oracle - that is calculated how much Ant is needed to cover it - and then this gets returned to the client/lib so what comes back with the quote is the estimated ANT for the full shebang. And then the PM takes the max fee, and then refund anything that's excess after the transaction goes through. So there's a refund of any unused portion to the mark account, with only the real amount of ANT deducted.
🔥 1
View in Discord →

#🌐︱general-chat 20:11:28 davidmc0 → replied to JimCollinson
jimcollinson said:
But then the cascade failure modes of a swing between demand positive to data drought, which we talk ... [truncated]
"But we can address things through an inflow of data in both cases… which is what we are all focused on of course!" Absolutely; getting inflow of data through demand is primary. It's also good to hear about the other issues you have considered and modelled. "cascade failure modes of a swing between demand positive to data drought". This is one I questioned recently here: https://forum.autonomi.community/t/nov-4-update-from-bux/42556/114?u=davidmc0 and have discussed it at other times as well. ...my question is whether a 'data drought' is realistic once demand is established given the diverse use cases expected for the network (general backup & sync, photo backup, video sharing, social media, websites etc etc). Are there examples of when web traffic, e.g. internet video uploads / cloud storage traffic all had a significant drop in demand for a prolonged time? I expect that, as the concept of node supply being unresponsive to increases in demand has been shown to be unrealistic, "demand droughts" that are significant enough to cause issues are unlikely to exist once demand is established.
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 20:11:39 davidmc0 → replied to JimCollinson
jimcollinson said:
But then the cascade failure modes of a swing between demand positive to data drought, which we talk ... [truncated]
Two more relevant points beyond questioning whether data droughts are likely to occur are: 1) Even if such a drought occurred, the problem causing a drought would be low demand, not insufficient supply, so the supply-stimulating emissions wouldn't help the problem of low demand. Maybe a demand-stimulating intervention would be better suited to that. 2) While node supply is elastic to demand increases (new nodes join quickly when store-cost is elevated), it's likely that it'll be less elastic to demand decreases, as operators leave them running hoping for future increases, or maintaining their share of the network revenues anticipating future token value increases. Basically, operators will have an incentive to add new nodes when demand is high, and an incentive to maintain nodes when demand is weak. If this turns out to be true, then during any temporary slow-down in demand, it's unlikely that a cascading failure due to node exits will happen. "Nor the reality of a cold start." Interesting. What are the concerns about a 'cold start' without emissions? I expect the community would provide sufficient nodes to get up and running ahead of demand growing that pool organically as data payments incentivise. Saying that, I'm sure the test of huge numbers of nodes enabled by emissions has been beneficial. Good to hear some of the thinking around economics, but yes, in the end, it'll all be good if there is decent demand, and it'll be bad if there isn't 🙂
View in Discord →

I've given up trying to help with a workable solution that encourages healthy nodes, not having collapse, and good numbers holding good data and discourage nodes just being added for numbers and profit without storage. I find it insulting this sort of response that it has not been done. And shows no one is really listening. Also insulting that people are **only** after zero emissions, I think that was suggested once by someone. 🤦 So many predicted this happening and again we know what will be happening if no one works on a more dynamic emissions solution. So much good work being done by the team only to be scuttled by a desire to have max numbers of nodes rather than max good nodes even if network is 1/2 to 3/4rds the size.

For instance, the Team section of the website could be replaced with content that highlights the Foundation and provides clearer information on how new participants can get involved in the project. 📅 Friday, 2025-11-21
#🌐︱general-chat 04:57:50 dimitarsafenetworkbulgaria → replied to JimCollinson
jimcollinson said:
on top of that there was a bug in the client which were no patching up which exacerbated the symptom ... [truncated]
I noticed and reported this exact bug because I always test-download everything I upload. I use this procedure for 100% of my uploads: if something doesn’t upload correctly the first time, I upload it again and test the download once more. This removes any false positives. So when I check again in the following days and some files fail to download, the problem is not caused by this bug.
👍 1
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 07:53:00 jimcollinson
The bug in the client is that it produces only one replica, not three, but _thinks_ there are three, and says “success”, and you may get a successful download initially, but then the chunks drop off because the network isn’t starting from the expected 3. But we have a fix!
🎉 6

📅 Thursday, 2025-11-27
#🌐︱general-chat 10:07:25 traktion0_20257 → replied to JimCollinson
jimcollinson said:
just to confirm, were aiming to do a release this week that will fix the problem
fwiw, I think we need a stronger production mindset for the network. Imagine if there was data loss issues on Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, Youtube, etc, which went on for weeks. Compound that will little information coming out from the team, other than the occasional message like the above? How about Cloudflare? Would they have let issues drift on for days at a time, without giving advise on how to circumvent the issues for sites depending on them? At what point would regular production systems pivot to failover, if there was an ongoing issue? Within hours is likely, assuming reverting to the previous version was acceptable. Usually staged/phased roll outs are desirable too. Non-breaking changes are a requirement, to allow easy roll back, with changes made over a number of releases where that isn't possible. Is it possible to roll back the client libs? Is it possible to roll back nodes? Until these sort of things are taken seriously, the network isn't going to be considered for anything approaching production usage. Businesses can't just wait for weeks, even days, for these things to be corrected. I understand the network is young, complex, pushing new boundaries, etc. However, clear/simple failover/rollback plans should be part of every release, especially if it is only an issue with the client side. This comes from a place that cares. Please can we do more here.
👍 5
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 10:23:41 jimcollinson
Yes, I agree. And it's not to excuse, but we are making strides towards towards this.
👍 3 ❤️ 3
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 10:29:32 mightyfool0689 → replied to JimCollinson
jimcollinson said:
Yes, I agree. And it's not to excuse, but we are making strides towards towards this.
Good to hear! And it's such a fine line right? Having a production mindset is great and it will definitly help with how reliable the network is perceived. At the same time it massively slows releases down, something you may not want at this stage as it will then be perceived as if issues are not addressed in a timely manner. But yeah, I do think we're getting closer and closer to the network needing to have a production mindset. Basically once we have called data perpetuallity or wider adoption, whichever comes first really.
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 10:33:15 jimcollinson
Yes, but the communication around it is definitely on us. Obviously, we don't have the resources and team size of cloud flare, but we hear (and feel) the frustrations, so wanted to acknowledge that.
